skill.name: supabase-patterns
skill.description: Framework-agnostic Supabase guidance for Row Level Security, migrations, realtime subscriptions, storage, and secure client usage.
skill.summary: Apply security-first Supabase patterns for RLS policies, migrations, storage, realtime, and typed clients.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backticks, Windows SAM access, system reconnaissance, and weak cryptography are false positives caused by Markdown fences, SQL examples, and Supabase terminology. The skill is not malicious, but it includes Supabase CLI commands, file redirection, environment variable examples, and database policy migration guidance that can change projects if applied without review. A semantic review also found SECURITY DEFINER helper examples without explicit search_path hardening.

medium_findings.title: Unhardened SECURITY DEFINER Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The RLS cookbook defines SECURITY DEFINER helper functions without showing explicit search_path hardening. These are legitimate PostgreSQL examples, but copied patterns can create privilege escalation risk in real Supabase projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SECURITY DEFINER clauses are directly present in database security guidance. The risk depends on deployment details, but the missing hardening context is a recognized PostgreSQL safety concern.

medium_findings.title: Database Mutation Commands Require Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes Supabase CLI and migration examples, including remote database push guidance and policy replacement statements. These operations are expected for a Supabase skill, but they can alter project state if executed against the wrong environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and migration statements are explicit and operational. They are not malicious, but the safety impact depends on the target Supabase project and environment.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most external command alerts point to Markdown code fences or inline cookbook file references. They do not execute Ruby or shell commands by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation syntax and SQL fenced blocks. No executable wrapper or dynamic command construction is present in those locations.

low_findings.title: Supabase Environment Variable Example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The environment access examples read SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_ANON_KEY for client initialization. The skill also warns against placing service role keys in client-side code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited variables are standard Supabase client configuration values. The confidence is not higher because project frameworks may require different public environment variable naming.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Token Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: No evidence found for Windows SAM access, credential theft, or weak cryptographic algorithm use. The cited areas are Supabase descriptions, variables, SQL statements, and PostgreSQL function syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged contexts found SQL examples and frontmatter, not filesystem paths or cryptographic routines. No prompt injection text or malicious authority claims were observed.

dangerous_patterns.title: SECURITY DEFINER Without Hardening Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The cookbook recommends SECURITY DEFINER helper functions but does not show an explicit hardened search_path. Users should review and harden these functions before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in security-sensitive SQL examples. The severity is medium because it is guidance, not code that runs automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Database Push Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick reference includes a Supabase database push command. Users should confirm the active project and environment before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly documented and can affect a remote database. It is a normal Supabase operation, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

content.user_title: Secure Supabase Apps with RLS Patterns
content.value_statement: Supabase projects often fail because database access rules are added late or copied inconsistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reusable patterns for RLS, migrations, storage, realtime, and typed clients.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of Supabase Row Level Security policies for owned rows, public reads, roles, and teams.
Shows migration patterns for enabling RLS and replacing policies in a repeatable SQL file.
Provides Supabase CLI references for initialization, local development, database pushes, and type generation.
Documents client patterns for typed queries, inserts, realtime subscriptions, and storage uploads.
Lists production security checks for keys, storage policies, realtime exposure, CORS, and rate limits.
limitations: It provides generic Supabase guidance and does not inspect a project automatically.
It does not include framework-specific setup for Next.js, React Native, SvelteKit, or backend-only services.
CLI and migration examples can change project state and need environment review before execution.
Security definer examples need production hardening before direct reuse.

use_cases.title: Add RLS to a New Product Table
use_cases.description: Create owner-based SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE policies before exposing a Supabase table to clients.

use_cases.title: Review Supabase Access Rules
use_cases.description: Check migrations for missing WITH CHECK clauses, permissive authenticated policies, and tables without RLS enabled.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Typed Supabase Client Workflow
use_cases.description: Use generated database types with safe client queries, storage uploads, and realtime subscriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic RLS Policies
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a table owned by users.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Supabase patterns skill to design RLS policies for a posts table with user ownership. Include read, create, update, and delete behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Policies
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Supabase migration for RLS coverage. Identify missing WITH CHECK clauses, overly broad policies, and tables that need RLS enabled.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Team-Based Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Supabase RLS policies for team projects where members can read team records and admins can update them. Explain the assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Production Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these Supabase access rules into a production migration plan. Include RLS enablement, policy replacement order, testing steps, and SECURITY DEFINER hardening notes.

output_examples.input: Ask for RLS policies for user-owned posts.
output_examples.output: A concise policy plan that enables RLS, limits reads and writes to the owning user, and calls out required insert checks.

output_examples.input: Ask for a review of a migration with public and private content.
output_examples.output: A security review that separates public read access from owner access and flags broad authenticated-user policies.

output_examples.input: Ask for production readiness guidance before running Supabase migrations.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering active project verification, backup expectations, policy tests, generated types, and environment-specific risks.

best_practices: Enable RLS immediately when creating tables that are exposed through Supabase APIs.
Use WITH CHECK for inserts and updates so users cannot write rows for another identity.
Test policies with anonymous, normal user, owner, team member, and admin contexts.
anti_patterns: Do not put service role keys in client-side code or browser-visible configuration.
Do not rely on broad authenticated-user policies for sensitive tables.
Do not run database push or policy replacement commands without confirming the target project.

faq.question: Does this skill run Supabase commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents command patterns, but users should confirm the target project before execution.

faq.question: Is this skill specific to one JavaScript framework?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is framework-agnostic and focuses on Supabase, PostgreSQL, and client usage patterns.

faq.question: Can it create complete migrations?
faq.answer: It can guide migration structure and policy content, but migrations still need project-specific review and testing.

faq.question: Does it cover Supabase Storage?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes basic storage upload and public URL patterns, with reminders to use bucket policies.

faq.question: Does it cover realtime subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a subscription pattern and warns against exposing sensitive columns through realtime.

faq.question: What is the main security warning?
faq.answer: Review database mutation commands and harden SECURITY DEFINER functions before production use.
